Wrap it in [staticmethod()](https://docs.python.org/dev/library/functions.html#staticmethod) if you want to preserve the old behavior. (Contributed by Serhiy Storchaka and Dominykas Grigonis in [gh-121027](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/121027).)

This was reported downstream in Fedora as https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2322407.

It’s hard to test this change in a virtualenv since there are some dependencies like numpy that are nontrivial to compile from source and haven’t published binary wheels for Python 3.14. However, I tested this PR as a patch for nibabel 5.3.2 in Fedora’s early Python 3.14 testing and got a [successful build with no regressions](https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/g/python/python3.14/build/8315861/) using Python ### Context for the issue:

The change that is causing this is discussed in the [release notes for the upcoming SWIG 4.3.0 release](https://github.com/swig/swig/blob/ea8aa84b28d67e28c3a4c9a798af7077b7a02cb0/CHANGES.current#L352-L372):

```
2024-06-15: vadz
            [Python] #2907 Fix returning null from functions with output
            parameters.  Ensures OUTPUT and INOUT typemaps are handled
            consistently wrt return type.

            New declaration of SWIG_Python_AppendOutput is now:

              SWIG_Python_AppendOutput(PyObject* result, PyObject* obj, int is_void);

            The 3rd parameter is new and the new $isvoid special variable
            should be passed to it, indicating whether or not the wrapped
            function returns void.

            Also consider replacing with:

              SWIG_AppendOutput(PyObject* result, PyObject* obj);

            which calls SWIG_Python_AppendOutput with same parameters but adding $isvoid
            for final parameter.

	    *** POTENTIAL INCOMPATIBILITY ***
```

When I tried replacing each occurrence of `SWIG_Python_AppendOutput` with `SWIG_AppendOutput`, I was left with only the pre-existing errors reported in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27577. I plan to follow up with a PR.

See also [this additional discussion](https://sourceforge.net/p/swig/mailman/message/58825934/) on the `Swig-devel` mailing list.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27578/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27578/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Fixes #27578.

See #27578 for context. From SWIG upstream:

```
2024-06-15: vadz
            [Python] #2907 Fix returning null from functions with output
            parameters.  Ensures OUTPUT and INOUT typemaps are handled
            consistently wrt return type.

            New declaration of SWIG_Python_AppendOutput is now:

              SWIG_Python_AppendOutput(PyObject* result, PyObject* obj, int is_void);

            The 3rd parameter is new and the new $isvoid special variable
            should be passed to it, indicating whether or not the wrapped
            function returns void.

            Also consider replacing with:

              SWIG_AppendOutput(PyObject* result, PyObject* obj);

            which calls SWIG_Python_AppendOutput with same parameters but adding $isvoid
            for final parameter.

	    *** POTENTIAL INCOMPATIBILITY ***
```

I don’t understand SWIG very well, so I’ve assumed that a blind search-and-replace of `SWIG_Python_AppendOutput` with `SWIG_AppendOutput` at least does not make anything worse! Review by someone more familiar with SWIG would be welcome.

See also [related discussion on the `Swig-devel` mailing Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

<!-- Good things to put here include:
       - reasons for the change (please link any relevant issues!),
       - any noteworthy (or hacky) choices to be aware of,
       - or what the problem resolved here looked like. -->

In Fedora Rawhide (the development version), the system Python is now 3.13.0rc1. We are therefore testing conda (and everything else) on Python 3.13 regardless of whether “official” support is ready or not.

This PR fixes errors like:

```
+ conda info
Traceback (most recent call last):
  […]
  File "/builddir/build/BUILD/conda-24.7.1-build/BUILDROOT/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/conda/common/io.py", line 272, in _logger_lock
    logging._acquireLock()
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AttributeError: module 'logging' has no attribute '_acquireLock'
```

Currently in Python 3.13, the `logging` functions `_prepareFork` and `_afterFork` correspond closely to the old `_acquireLock` and `_releaseLock`, which have been removed.

As long as conda is already using non-public APIs, it’s easiest to just call *those* on Python 3.13. That approach could perhaps break in the future if these functions start doing something other than acquire/release a module lock, but copying their contents could *also* break if the internal locking semantics change in a future Python, so :shrug:.

(See also https://github.com/celery/billiard/pull/404.)

Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

<!-- Good things to put here include:
       - reasons for the change (please link any relevant issues!),
       - any noteworthy (or hacky) choices to be aware of,
       - or what the problem resolved here looked like. -->

In Fedora Rawhide (the development version), the system Python is now 3.13.0rc1. We are therefore testing conda (and everything else) on Python 3.13 regardless of whether “official” support is ready or not.

This PR fixes errors like:

```
+ conda info
Traceback (most recent call last):
  […]
  File "/builddir/build/BUILD/conda-24.7.1-build/BUILDROOT/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/conda/common/io.py", line 272, in _logger_lock
    logging._acquireLock()
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AttributeError: module 'logging' has no attribute '_acquireLock'
```

Currently in Python 3.13, the `logging` functions `_prepareFork` and `_afterFork` correspond closely to the old `_acquireLock` and `_releaseLock`, which have been removed.

As long as conda is already using non-public APIs, it’s easiest to just call *those* on Python 3.13. That approach could perhaps break in the future if these functions start doing something other than acquire/release a module lock, but copying their contents could *also* break if the internal locking semantics change in a future Python, so :shrug:.

(See also https://github.com/celery/billiard/pull/404.)

The `main` and `maintenance/2.0.x` branches already lack an upper bound on the `meson-python` version, e.g.:

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/50a705cb9c6f4c755c9793f719d35ebdfe574978/pyproject.toml#L4

I tested this in Fedora by building an [updated `python-meson-python` package](https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/python-meson-python/pull-request/4) and test-rebuilding the [`numpy` package](https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/numpy) with this PR applied as a `param` 2.0.0; nothing else matters here

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

Some of the documentation is installed in a `doc` directory in `site-packages`, as if it were a Python package.

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
python3.12 -m venv _e
. _e/bin/activate
pip install param
find _e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/
```

```
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/index.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/logging.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/param_namespace.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/parameter_helpers.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/parameterized_helpers.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/parameterized_objects.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/parameters.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/reactive.md
_e/lib/python3.12/site-packages/doc/reference/param/serialization.md
```

Alternatively, working in a clean git checkout:

```
python3 -m build
python3 -m wheel unpack dist/param-*.whl
find param-*/doc/
```

#### Stack traceback and/or browser JavaScript console output

N/A

#### Screenshots or screencasts of the bug in action

N/A

- [x] I may be interested in making a pull request to address this

I think this can be fixed by adding `exclude = ["doc"]` to `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]` in `pyproject.toml`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/877/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/877/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

## Description of Changes


* [ ] Wrote at least one-line docstrings (for any new functions) **N/A**
* [ ] Added unit test(s) covering the changes (if testable) **N/A**
<!--- Remember that an image/animation is worth a thousand words! --->
* [ ] Included a screenshot or animation (if affecting the UI, see [Licecap](https://www.cockos.com/licecap/)) **N/A**


<!--- Explain what you've done and why --->

Use the `name` element in a `developer` block instead, as recommended by `appstreamcli` 1.0.0.

This fixes all warnings when validating the AppData XML file, although there are still informational messages:

* [ ] Wrote at least one-line docstrings (for any new functions) **N/A**
* [ ] Added unit test(s) covering the changes (if testable) **N/A**
<!--- Remember that an image/animation is worth a thousand words! --->

<!--- Explain what you've done and why --->

Use the `name` element in a `developer` block instead, as recommended by `appstreamcli` 1.0.0.

This fixes all warnings when validating the AppData XML file, although there are still informational messages:

```
I: org.spyder_ide.spyder:32: developer-id-missing
   The `developer` element is missing an `id` property, containing a unique string ID for the
   developer. Consider adding a unique ID.

I: org.spyder_ide.spyder:~: content-rating-missing
   This component has no `content_rating` tag to provide age rating information. You can generate
   the tag data online by answering a few questions at https://hughsie.github.io/oars/

✔ Validation was successful: infos: 2, pedantic: 2
```

It documents the `id` property of the `developer` element as follows:

> The element should have a id property, containing a unique ID to identify the respective developer. It is recommended to use a web URL, like gnome.org as ID to achieve a higher chance of uniqueness.

How about I write:

```xml
<developer id="spyder-ide.org">
```

That would seem to fit with the spirit of the design and eliminate one of the warnings.

<!--- Explain what you've done and why --->

Use the `name` element in a `developer` block instead, as recommended by `appstreamcli` 1.0.0.

This fixes all warnings when validating the AppData XML file, although there are still informational messages:

```
I: org.spyder_ide.spyder:32: developer-id-missing
   The `developer` element is missing an `id` property, containing a unique string ID for the
   developer. Consider adding a unique ID.

I: org.spyder_ide.spyder:~: content-rating-missing
   This component has no `content_rating` tag to provide age rating information. You can generate
   the tag data online by answering a few questions at https://hughsie.github.io/oars/

✔ Validation was successful: infos: 2, pedantic: 2
```

After some packaging changes (mostly welcome simplifications!) I’m able to build `nipy` in Fedora Rawhide with:

- Python 3.12.0 (final)
- NumPy 1.26.0
- All tests, including doctests, running and passing with `pytest` and no `nose` or `nose3`

I’m going to keep fussing over packaging details for a while, try things on non-`x86_64` architectures, maybe offer some PR’s to deal with some warnings, and so on, before actually updating the Fedora package. Still, the recent work on this project has been an amazingly successful sprint.

Feel free to close this issue any time you like.

By the way, are you expecting to tag a new release in the moderately near future?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/issues/comments/1759978210/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

Test collection in `pytest-xdist` may fail due to inconsistent parameterization order in `test_b_orthonormalize`.

The problem is that `test_b_orthonormalize` is parameterized over `REAL_DTYPES`,

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L103-L105

which is a set:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L24

The `pytest-xdist` documentation [explains why this is a problem](https://pytest-xdist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/known-limitations.html#order-and-amount-of-test-must-be-consistent), and the surrounding context shows that this is known:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L22-L28

There are a couple of questions I have before opening a PR to fix this:

- Is `REAL_DTYPES` (vs. say `VDTYPES`) indeed the correct set of types over which to parameterize all three of `Vdtype`, `Bdtype`, and `BVdtype`?
- What would be an appropriate name for a sorted-tuple version of `REAL_DTYPES` to go alongside `VDTYPES` and `MDTYPES`?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
Simply build and run the tests—but the failure seems to be environment-dependent and is not guaranteed to be reproducible. This doesn’t matter, as the root cause can be found by inspection of the relevant test code.
```

Describe your issue.

Test collection in `pytest-xdist` may fail due to inconsistent parameterization order in `test_b_orthonormalize`.

The problem is that `test_b_orthonormalize` is parameterized over `REAL_DTYPES`,

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L103-L105

which is a set:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L24

The `pytest-xdist` documentation [explains why this is a problem](https://pytest-xdist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/known-limitations.html#order-and-amount-of-test-must-be-consistent), and the surrounding context shows that this is known:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L22-L28

There are a couple of questions I have before opening a PR to fix this:

- Is `REAL_DTYPES` (vs. say `VDTYPES`) indeed the correct set of types over which to parameterize all three of `Vdtype`, `Bdtype`, and `BVdtype`?
- What would be an appropriate name for a sorted-tuple version of `REAL_DTYPES` to go alongside `VDTYPES` and `MDTYPES`?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
Simply build and run the tests—but the failure seems to be environment-dependent and is not guaranteed to be reproducible. This doesn’t matter, as the root cause can be found by inspection of the relevant test code.
```

If this is a problem, it better be fixed everywhere.

I looked for other instances of the same problem. I didn’t find anywhere else in `main` that a set (or dict) was used for a parameterized/“parametrized” test.

I found some cases like this, which are OK because the order is consistent:

```
REAL_DTYPES = [float32, float64]

[…]
    @pytest.mark.parametrize("FOOdtype", REAL_DTYPES) 
```

And something like this is not as good—and should be changed—because the test execution will vary based on set iteration order, but it wouldn’t cause an error with `pytest-xdist`. However, I didn’t find any actual cases like this.

```
REAL_DTYPES = {float32, float64}

[…]
    for i, dt in enumerate(REAL_DTYPES):
        # do something that depends on i and dt
        assert_allclose(...)
```

And finally, there are some very similar cases where a consistently-ordered tuple or list is formed from a set before using it to paramet(e)rize a test, e.g.:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/csgraph/tests/test_graph_laplacian.py#L163-L167

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/csgraph/tests/test_graph_laplacian.py#L316

So far, `test_b_orthonormalize` is the only one I found that uses a set directly for paramet(e)rization without first forming a consistently-ordered tuple or list.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1783271433/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

Test collection in `pytest-xdist` may fail due to inconsistent parameterization order in `test_b_orthonormalize`.

The problem is that `test_b_orthonormalize` is parameterized over `REAL_DTYPES`,

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L103-L105

which is a set:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L24

The `pytest-xdist` documentation [explains why this is a problem](https://pytest-xdist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/known-limitations.html#order-and-amount-of-test-must-be-consistent), and the surrounding context shows that this is known:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L22-L28

There are a couple of questions I have before opening a PR to fix this:

#### Reference issue

Closes https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19442.
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

#### What does this implement/fix?

This fixes https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19442 minimally by parameterizing `test_b_orthonormalize` over a sorted-tuple version of the set `REAL_DVALUES`.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
As reported in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19442#issuecomment-1783523842, I tried paramet(e)rizing over `VDTYPES` instead, but the tests involving complex dtypes failed.

Describe your issue.

Test collection in `pytest-xdist` may fail due to inconsistent parameterization order in `test_b_orthonormalize`.

The problem is that `test_b_orthonormalize` is parameterized over `REAL_DTYPES`,

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L103-L105

which is a set:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L24

The `pytest-xdist` documentation [explains why this is a problem](https://pytest-xdist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/known-limitations.html#order-and-amount-of-test-must-be-consistent), and the surrounding context shows that this is known:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L22-L28

There are a couple of questions I have before opening a PR to fix this:

- Is `REAL_DTYPES` (vs. say `VDTYPES`) indeed the correct set of types over which to parameterize all three of `Vdtype`, `Bdtype`, and `BVdtype`?
- What would be an appropriate name for a sorted-tuple version of `REAL_DTYPES` to go alongside `VDTYPES` and `MDTYPES`?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
Simply build and run the tests—but the failure seems to be environment-dependent and is not guaranteed to be reproducible. This doesn’t matter, as the root cause can be found by inspection of the relevant test code.
```

Describe your issue.

Test collection in `pytest-xdist` may fail due to inconsistent parameterization order in `test_b_orthonormalize`.

The problem is that `test_b_orthonormalize` is parameterized over `REAL_DTYPES`,

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L103-L105

which is a set:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L24

The `pytest-xdist` documentation [explains why this is a problem](https://pytest-xdist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/known-limitations.html#order-and-amount-of-test-must-be-consistent), and the surrounding context shows that this is known:

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f990b1d2471748c79bc4260baf8923db0a5248af/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L22-L28

There are a couple of questions I have before opening a PR to fix this:

- Is `REAL_DTYPES` (vs. say `VDTYPES`) indeed the correct set of types over which to parameterize all three of `Vdtype`, `Bdtype`, and `BVdtype`?
- What would be an appropriate name for a sorted-tuple version of `REAL_DTYPES` to go alongside `VDTYPES` and `MDTYPES`?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
Simply build and run the tests—but the failure seems to be environment-dependent and is not guaranteed to be reproducible. This doesn’t matter, as the root cause can be found by inspection of the relevant test code.
```

Describe your issue.

In

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/eff7bfd88a6b92f0e213319628d222ca0116db7c/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L104-L106

changing any of the `REAL_DTYPES` to `VDTYPES` as suggested in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19442#issuecomment-1783399099 causes the tests to fail.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("Vdtype", VDTYPES) 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("Bdtype", VDTYPES) 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("BVdtype", VDTYPES)
```

Describe your issue.

In

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/eff7bfd88a6b92f0e213319628d222ca0116db7c/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L104-L106

changing any of the `REAL_DTYPES` to `VDTYPES` as suggested in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19442#issuecomment-1783399099 causes the tests to fail.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("Vdtype", VDTYPES) 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("Bdtype", VDTYPES) 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("BVdtype", VDTYPES)
```

Describe your issue.

In

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/eff7bfd88a6b92f0e213319628d222ca0116db7c/scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/lobpcg/tests/test_lobpcg.py#L104-L106

changing any of the `REAL_DTYPES` to `VDTYPES` as suggested in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19442#issuecomment-1783399099 causes the tests to fail.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("Vdtype", VDTYPES) 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("Bdtype", VDTYPES) 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("BVdtype", VDTYPES)
```

{"action":"opened","number":510,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/pulls/510","id":1524926658,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAAnNKM5a5IjC","html_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nipy/pull/510","diff_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nipy/pull/510.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nipy/pull/510.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/issues/510","number":510,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Replace get_data() with get_fdata()","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This seems to fix test errors and failures due to:

```
nibabel.deprecator.ExpiredDeprecationError: get_data() is deprecated in favor of get_fdata(), which has a more predictable return type. To obtain get_data() behavior going forward, use numpy.asanyarray(img.dataobj).
* deprecated from version: 3.0
* Raises <class 'nibabel.deprecator.ExpiredDeprecationError'> as of version: 5.0
```

This PR reflects a totally oblivious search-and-replace. It’s entirely possible that this is not the right thing to do in all cases, but I’m not able to work on a more fastidious fix at this time. Nevertheless, it does fix most of the current test failures and errors.

See:

I wonder, is there some incompatibility with Python 3.12?

We don’t have `nose3` in Fedora (https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Changes/DeprecateNose happened, and `nose3` conflicts with it since it still provides `import nose`), so this is with a patched copy of old-school `nose`.

I’ll guess I could see if I can reproduce it in a virtualenv, or with a hacked-together local `nose3` RPM package, or something.

The biggest obstacle I have right now is that I am having a hard time establishing exactly where the traceback is coming from.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/issues/comments/1730238013/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Synchronized `python-lsp-server` and bumped the `jedi` upper bound from `<0.19.0` to `<0.20.0` (supported in `python-lsp-server` since 1.8.0), as described in https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/issues/21296#issuecomment-1699382983.

## Problem Description
spyder currently sets an upper bound on its jedi dependency of 0.19.0, in several files - `binder/environment.yml`, `external-deps/python-lsp-server/pyproject.toml`, `requirements/main.yml`, `setup.py` and `spyder/dependencies.py` at least. Upstream lsp, which is where this dependency comes from I believe, [bumped it to 0.20.0 recently](https://github.com/python-lsp/python-lsp-server/pull/416) with the note that there "don't seem to be any breaking changes". spyder should probably inherit this bump so it can be used with jedi 0.19.


### What steps reproduce the problem?

1. Try and install spyder with jedi 0.19.

### What is the expected output? What do you see instead?

It fails, but it should be allowed.

## Versions

<!--- https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md --->
<!--- and follow PEP 8, PEP 257 and Spyder's code style:               --->

## Description of Changes

Removes shebang lines (`#!`) from three non-executable scripts within the `spyder/` package, and sets the executable bit on `tools/rm_whitespace.py`.

* [ ] Wrote at least one-line docstrings (for any new functions) **N/A**
* [ ] Added unit test(s) covering the changes (if testable) **N/A**
<!--- Remember that an image/animation is worth a thousand words! --->
* [ ] Included a screenshot or animation (if affecting the UI, see [Licecap](https://www.cockos.com/licecap/)) **N/A**


<!--- Explain what you've done and why --->

There is no value in having a shebang (`#!`) line in a file that does not have the executable bit set in its filesystem permissions, even if it is script-like (has a “main routine” or other interesting side effects).

Accordingly, shebang lines are removed from three non-executable files within the `spyder/` package.

For `tools/rm_whitespace.py`, I chose to make it executable instead of removing the shebang line. However, the opposite approach of removing the shebang and expecting people to continue to run it as `python tools/rm_whitespace.py` is reasonable too, and I’m happy to amend the PR if that’s preferred.


### Issue(s) Resolved

<!--- List the issue(s) below, in the form "Fixes #1234"; one per line --->

**N/A**

## Description of Changes

Removes shebang lines (`#!`) from three non-executable scripts within the `spyder/` package, and sets the executable bit on `tools/rm_whitespace.py`.

* [ ] Wrote at least one-line docstrings (for any new functions) **N/A**
* [ ] Added unit test(s) covering the changes (if testable) **N/A**
<!--- Remember that an image/animation is worth a thousand words! --->
* [ ] Included a screenshot or animation (if affecting the UI, see [Licecap](https://www.cockos.com/licecap/)) **N/A**


<!--- Explain what you've done and why --->

There is no value in having a shebang (`#!`) line in a file that does not have the executable bit set in its filesystem permissions, even if it is script-like (has a “main routine” or other interesting side effects).

Accordingly, shebang lines are removed from three non-executable files within the `spyder/` package.

For `tools/rm_whitespace.py`, I chose to make it executable instead of removing the shebang line. However, the opposite approach of removing the shebang and expecting people to continue to run it as `python tools/rm_whitespace.py` is reasonable too, and I’m happy to amend the PR if that’s preferred.


### Issue(s) Resolved

<!--- List the issue(s) below, in the form "Fixes #1234"; one per line --->

**N/A**

I tried applying the linked PR, and got a lot of errors like:

```
nibabel.deprecator.ExpiredDeprecationError: get_data() is deprecated in favor of get_fdata(), which has a more predictable return type. To obtain get_data() behavior going forward, use numpy.asanyarray(img.dataobj).
* deprecated from version: 3.0
* Raises <class 'nibabel.deprecator.ExpiredDeprecationError'> as of version: 5.0
```

As an experiment, I tried a totally blind search and replace,

```
find . -type f -name '*.py' -print0 |
  xargs -r -0 -t sed -r -i 's/\bget_data\b/get_fdata/g'
```

and found that got me down to nine errors. nsigned int]: Assertion '__n < this->size()' failed.

```

Compare to

```
scons build -j16

```

which succeeds.

- Cantera version: c0ae563def4ba5a91e995194e2ec9d30a1413671

ALL software version info

`param` 1.12.2 + https://github.com/holoviz/param/pull/638
Python 3.11.0b3 (pre-release)

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

Tests fail on Python 3.11.0b3 because the new async support in `param/_async.py` and its tests use `asyncio.coroutine`, which has been deprecated since Python 3.8 and is [removed in Python 3.11](https://docs.python.org/3.11/whatsnew/3.11.html#removed).

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
gh repo clone param/param
cd param
git checkout 2958f6327ad3a5222eb773801e3e46f6130d9f65
python3.11 -m venv _e
. _e/bin/activate
pip install -e .[tests]
pytest
```

#### Stack traceback and/or browser JavaScript console output

```
====================================================== FAILURES ======================================================
____________________________________________ TestParamDepends.test_async _____________________________________________

self = <tests.API1.testparamdepends.TestParamDepends testMethod=test_async>

    @pytest.mark.skipif(sys.version_info.major == 2, reason='asyncio only on Python 3')
    def test_async(self):
        try:
            param.parameterized.async_executor = async_executor
>           class P(param.Parameterized):

tests/API1/testparamdepends.py:684: 
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 

    class P(param.Parameterized):

ALL software version info

`param` 1.12.2 + https://github.com/holoviz/param/pull/638
Python 3.11.0b3 (pre-release)

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

Tests fail on Python 3.11.0b3 because the new async support in `param/_async.py` and its tests use `asyncio.coroutine`, which has been deprecated since Python 3.8 and is [removed in Python 3.11](https://docs.python.org/3.11/whatsnew/3.11.html#removed).

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
gh repo clone param/param
cd param
git checkout 2958f6327ad3a5222eb773801e3e46f6130d9f65
python3.11 -m venv _e
. _e/bin/activate
pip install -e .[tests]
pytest
```

,"merged_at":"2022-09-01T12:08:59Z"},"body":"<!--
Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.

3.11","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-08-02T04:43:49Z","updated_at":"2022-08-02T04:43:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Because the `annotations` feature was postponed indefinitely, `__future__.annotations.getMandatoryRelease()` returns `None` in Python 3.11, which [means a planned feature got dropped](https://docs.python.org/3.11/library/__future__.html).

As a result, importing `patsy.eval` fails with a traceback like:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/ben/src/forks/patsy/patsy/__init__.py", line 77, in <module>
    import patsy.highlevel
  File "/home/ben/src/forks/patsy/patsy/highlevel.py", line 19, in <module>
    from patsy.design_info import DesignMatrix, DesignInfo
  File "/home/ben/src/forks/patsy/patsy/design_info.py", line 38, in <module>
    from patsy.desc import ModelDesc, Term
  File "/home/ben/src/forks/patsy/patsy/desc.py", line 14, in <module>
    from patsy.eval import EvalEnvironment, EvalFactor
  File "/home/ben/src/forks/patsy/patsy/eval.py", line 36, in <module>
    _ALL_FUTURE_FLAGS = _all_future_flags()
                        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/ben/src/forks/patsy/patsy/eval.py", line 32, in _all_future_flags
    if feature.getMandatoryRelease() > sys.version_info:
       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'sys.version_info'
```

PR to follow.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/patsy/issues/186/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/patsy/issues/186/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.

- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/events","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110","id":1243963499,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAbcwm844O4Qk","number":9110,"title":"Add `pandas[test]` to `test` extra","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2022-05-21T12:24:17Z","updated_at":"2022-07-22T17:10:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/9110","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Since the tests use `pandas.test` (the actual Pandas test suite), not only `pandas.testing`, it makes sense to rely on the Pandas `test` extra.

- [x] Closes (no issue filed)
- [x] Tests added / passed
- [x] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

This is mostly a formality, but it could possibly avoid any practical issues with new Pandas test dependencies in the future.

[Currently](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1be9d3868f4b9cfd083839cf39eecfd881f387ad/setup.cfg#L48), this change brings in:

```ini
[options.extras_require]
test =
    hypothesis>=5.5.3
    pytest>=6.0
    pytest-xdist>=1.31
```

which adds minimum versions to the `pytest` and `pytest-xdist` dependencies Dask’s tests already have, and a (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependency on `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not I’m the maintainer of the [`python-param` package](https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/python-param) in Fedora Linux, and we’re moving to Python 3.11 in our development version (Rawhide) now, so this is [already broken for us](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2093926), with errors like:

```
_______________ ERROR collecting tests/API0/testdynamicparams.py _______________
tests/API0/testdynamicparams.py:14: in <module>
    import numbergen
numbergen/__init__.py:277: in <module>
    class TimeAwareRandomState(TimeAware):
numbergen/__init__.py:310: in TimeAwareRandomState
    default=random.Random((500,500)), doc=
/usr/lib64/python3.11/random.py:125: in __init__
    self.seed(x)
/usr/lib64/python3.11/random.py:160: in seed
    raise TypeError('The only supported seed types are: None,\n'
E   TypeError: The only supported seed types are: None,
E   int, float, str, bytes, and bytearray.
```

To understand how `(500, 500)` becomes a RNG state, we can check the CPython source code [in Modules/_randommodule.c](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/f9585e2adc36fb2886f96ef86a5aa14215fc1151/Modules/_randommodule.c#L299):

```c
static PyObject *
random_seed(RandomObject *self, PyObject *arg)
{
    PyObject *result = NULL;            /* guilty until proved innocent */
    PyObject *n = NULL;
    /* [... snip ... ] */

    if (arg == NULL || arg == Py_None) {
        /* [... snip ... ] */
    }

    /* This algorithm relies on the number being unsigned.
     * So: if the arg is a PyLong, use its absolute value.
     * Otherwise use its hash value, cast to unsigned.
     */
    if (PyLong_CheckExact(arg)) {
        n = PyNumber_Absolute(arg);
    } else if (PyLong_Check(arg)) {
        /* [... snip ... ] */
    }
    else {
        Py_hash_t hash = PyObject_Hash(arg);
        if (hash == -1)
            goto Done;
        n = PyLong_FromSize_t((size_t)hash);
    }
    /* [... snip ... ] */
}
```

Thus for a non-numeric seed like this, the actual seed is its Python `hash()`, cast C-style to a `size_t`. That is:

```python
from random import Random
from ctypes import c_size_t

seed = (500, 500)
a = Random(seed)
b = Random(c_size_t(hash(seed)).value)

assert a.getstate() == b.getstate()
assert a.random() == b.random()
```

So we could explicitly continue the current behavior on Python 3.11 by writing:

```python
from ctypes import c_size_t
```

and then

```python
        default=random.Random(c_size_t(hash((500,500))).value), doc=
```

-----

As a side note, a big part of the reason that using arbitrary objects as seeds was removed is that [string hashes are randomized](https://peps.python.org/pep-0456/) in Python, so using hashable objects that contain strings can lead to surprising behavior, e.g.:

```bash
$ python3.10 -c 'from random import Random; print(Random(("500", "500")).random())'
0.543252271025492
$ python3.10 -c 'from random import Random; print(Random(("500", "500")).random())'
0.48946932166185464
```

The hash of a tuple-of-numbers like `(500, 500)` is still repeatable, though.

-----

One caveat about the way `Random` is seeded here is that the result will be, and *already is*, different on 64-bit and 32-bit platforms.

**64-bit**:

```
$ python3.10 -c 'from random import Random; print(Random((500, 500)).random())'
0.1248426853025093
```

**32-bit**:

```
$ python3.10 -c 'from random import Random; print(Random((500, 500)).random())'
0.7817896725813163
```

It’s also different between newer and older Pythons, as the hash algorithm has changed:

```
$ python2.7 -c 'from random import Random; print(Random((500, 500)).random())'
0.22241681053
```

So if we are being strict about preserving the same behavior, `c_size_t(hash((500,500))).value` cannot be simplified at all. On the other hand, if you wrote `13594734499543488215`, then all the other cases (Python 2.7, 32-bit builds) would change to match what you currently see on 64-bit builds of supported versions of Python 3.

-----

What do you think? I’m happy to follow up with a PR.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/comments/1155147795/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I don't remember the history, but in any case, my vote is for the number to be a specific integer that matches the number we used in the era when topographica was written. I.e., whatever that mapped to for the last Topographic release would be the right number to use now, to allow our older scientific results to be reproduced even if we updated to use recent versions of Param.

Looking at the [topographica source code](https://github.com/ioam/topographica/blob/1e097e2df9938a6ce9f48cefbf25672cbbf9a4db/setup.py#L169), it looks like it only ever supported Python 2.7. So maintaining forward-compatibility for topographica users via a hard-coded integer would mean using a hash value from Python 2.7—although it would still be necessary to pick 64-bit or 32-bit and hope that matched what users were running on. On the other hand, this would change the initial state for users on 64-bit Python 3, which should be the most common target today. And how long does `param` plan to continue supporting Python 2.7?

If a 2.0 release is on the horizon, maybe the right thing to do would be to use a computed integer `c_size_t(hash((500,500))).value` for now to maximize compatibility (as long as users are still using the same Python version and word size), and plan to introduce a fixed integer seed in 2.0 to remove the inconsistency across interpreters?

Just some thoughts—I don’t have a stake in any particular approach.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/comments/1156422806/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`param` 1.12.2 + https://github.com/holoviz/param/pull/638
Python 3.11.0b3 (pre-release)

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

Tests fail on Python 3.11.0b3 because the new async support in `param/_async.py` and its tests use `asyncio.coroutine`, which has been deprecated since Python 3.8 and is [removed in Python 3.11](https://docs.python.org/3.11/whatsnew/3.11.html#removed).

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
gh repo clone param/param
cd param
git checkout 2958f6327ad3a5222eb773801e3e46f6130d9f65
python3.11 -m venv _e
. _e/bin/activate
pip install -e .[tests]
pytest
```

#### Stack traceback and/or browser JavaScript console output

{"action":"opened","number":9110,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/9110","id":943424548,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAbcwm844O4Qk","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110","number":9110,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add “pandas[test]” to “test” extra","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Since the tests use `pandas.test` (the actual Pandas test suite), not only `pandas.testing`, it makes sense to rely on the Pandas `test` extra.

- [x] Closes (no issue filed)
- [x] Tests added / passed
- [x] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

This is mostly a formality, but it could possibly avoid any practical issues with new Pandas test dependencies in the future.

[Currently](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1be9d3868f4b9cfd083839cf39eecfd881f387ad/setup.cfg#L48), this change brings in:

```ini
[options.extras_require]
test =
    hypothesis>=5.5.3
    pytest>=6.0
    pytest-xdist>=1.31
```

which adds minimum versions to the `pytest` and `pytest-xdist` dependencies Dask’s tests already have, and a (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependency on `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not Bug reports that follow these guidelines are easier to diagnose, and so are often handled much more quickly.

**What happened**:

The GitHub source contains `dask/array/NUMPY_LICENSE.txt`. This makes sense, because some code is copied or forked from numpy, and under the BSD license terms this license and copyright text must be reproduced with distributions in source or binary form.

However, the PyPI sdist and bdist/wheel files do not contain this license file.

**What you expected to happen**:

PyPI distributions contain `dask/array/NUMPY_LICENSE.txt`.

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```bash
$ curl -L https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/d5/61/2ab5b87c89a91e96aa2039722b60570d4cae4961afa47a8f3dacc66bbcd9/dask-2022.5.0.tar.gz | tar -tzv | grep -F NUMPY_LICENSE.txt
# (no output)
$ python3 -m venv _e
$ . _e/bin/activate
(_e) $ pip install dask
Collecting dask
# […]
Successfully installed cloudpickle-2.1.0 dask-2022.5.0 fsspec-2022.5.0 locket-1.0.0 packaging-21.3 partd-1.2.0 pyparsing-3.0.9 pyyaml-6.0 toolz-0.11.2
(_e) $ find _e/lib/python3.10/site-packages/dask* -name NUMPY_LICENSE.txt
# (no output)
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:

It should be sufficient to add `dask/array/NUMPY_LICENSE.txt` to `metadata.license_files` in `setup.cfg`. I will follow up with a PR.

**Environment**:

- Dask version: any (2022.5.0, or current `main`)
- Python version: any (3.10.4)
- Operating System: any (Fedora Linux 36)
- Install method (conda, pip, source): any except GitHub source","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9112/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9112/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Bug reports that follow these guidelines are easier to diagnose, and so are often handled much more quickly.

**What happened**:

The PyPI sdist contains tests (`dask/tests/`), but it lacks `conftest.py`, so a number of tests fail that should not. For example, tests that use the `shuffle_method` fixture, fail with:

```
E       fixture 'shuffle_method' not found
```

**What you expected to happen**:

If PyPI sdists contain tests, then they should contain `conftest.py`.

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```bash
$ curl -L -O https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/d5/61/2ab5b87c89a91e96aa2039722b60570d4cae4961afa47a8f3dacc66bbcd9/dask-2022.5.0.tar.gz
$ tar -xzf dask-2022.5.0.tar.gz
$ python3 -m venv _e
$ . _e/bin/activate
(_e) $ cd dask-2022.5.0/
(_e) $ pip install -e ".[complete,test]"
[…]
Successfully installed MarkupSafe-2.1.1 attrs-21.4.0 bokeh-2.4.3 cfgv-3.3.1 click-8.1.3 cloudpickle-2.1.0 dask-2022.5.0 distlib-0.3.4 distributed-2022.5.0 execnet-1.9.0 filelock-3.7.0 fsspec-2022.5.0 heapdict-1.0.1 identify-2.5.1 iniconfig-1.1.1 jinja2-3.1.2 locket-1.0.0 msgpack-1.0.3 nodeenv-1.6.0 numpy-1.22.4 packaging-21.3 pandas-1.4.2 partd-1.2.0 pillow-9.1.1 platformdirs-2.5.2 pluggy-1.0.0 pre-commit-2.19.0 psutil-5.9.1 py-1.11.0 pyparsing-3.0.9 pytest-7.1.2 pytest-forked-1.4.0 pytest-rerunfailures-10.2 pytest-xdist-2.5.0 python-dateutil-2.8.2 pytz-2022.1 pyyaml-6.0 six-1.16.0 sortedcontainers-2.4.0 tblib-1.7.0 toml-0.10.2 tomli-2.0.1 toolz-0.11.2 tornado-6.1 typing-extensions-4.2.0 urllib3-1.26.9 virtualenv-20.14.1 zict-2.2.0
(_e) $ pytest
[…]
=========== 1 failed, 7092 passed, 875 skipped, 88 xfailed, 2691 warnings, 965 errors in 501.68s (0:08:21) ===========
# Note 965 errors due to missing conftest.py
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:

This is relevant to Linux distribution packaging, which wants to build wheels from source and run the tests, but (at least in Fedora Linux) which would prefer to use PyPI sdists instead of GitHub archives where possible.

It should be sufficient to add `onftest.py` to `MANIFEST.in`. I will follow up with a PR.

**Environment**:

- Dask version: any (2022.5.0, or current `main`)
- Python version: any (3.10.4)
- Operating System: any (Fedora Linux 36)
- Install method (conda, pip, source): PyPI sdist

{"action":"opened","number":2438,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/pulls/2438","id":943670470,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAKfjv844P0TG","html_url":"https://github.com/geopandas/geopandas/pull/2438","diff_url":"https://github.com/geopandas/geopandas/pull/2438.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/geopandas/geopandas/pull/2438.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/issues/2438","number":2438,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Depend on pandas[test] for testing","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Since geopandas imports parts of Pandas’s actual test suite (`pandas.tests`), not only `pandas.testing`, its tests ought to depend on the `test` extra of Pandas.

This is mostly a formality, but it could possibly avoid any practical issues with new Pandas test dependencies in the future.

[Currently](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1be9d3868f4b9cfd083839cf39eecfd881f387ad/setup.cfg#L48), this change brings in:

```ini
[options.extras_require]
test =
    hypothesis>=5.5.3
    pytest>=6.0
    pytest-xdist>=1.31
```

which adds a stricter minimum versions to the `pytest` dependency Geopandas’s tests already have, and (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependencies on `pytest-xdist` and `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not Bug reports that follow these guidelines are easier to diagnose, and so are often handled much more quickly.

**What happened**:

The PyPI sdist contains tests (`dask/tests/`), but it lacks `conftest.py`, so a number of tests fail that should not. For example, tests that use the `shuffle_method` fixture, fail with:

```
E       fixture 'shuffle_method' not found
```

**What you expected to happen**:

If PyPI sdists contain tests, then they should contain `conftest.py`.

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```bash
$ curl -L -O https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/d5/61/2ab5b87c89a91e96aa2039722b60570d4cae4961afa47a8f3dacc66bbcd9/dask-2022.5.0.tar.gz
$ tar -xzf dask-2022.5.0.tar.gz
$ python3 -m venv _e
$ . _e/bin/activate
(_e) $ cd dask-2022.5.0/
(_e) $ pip install -e ".[complete,test]"
[…]
Successfully installed MarkupSafe-2.1.1 attrs-21.4.0 bokeh-2.4.3 cfgv-3.3.1 click-8.1.3 cloudpickle-2.1.0 dask-2022.5.0 distlib-0.3.4 distributed-2022.5.0 execnet-1.9.0 filelock-3.7.0 fsspec-2022.5.0 heapdict-1.0.1 identify-2.5.1 iniconfig-1.1.1 jinja2-3.1.2 locket-1.0.0 msgpack-1.0.3 nodeenv-1.6.0 numpy-1.22.4 packaging-21.3 pandas-1.4.2 partd-1.2.0 pillow-9.1.1 platformdirs-2.5.2 pluggy-1.0.0 pre-commit-2.19.0 psutil-5.9.1 py-1.11.0 pyparsing-3.0.9 pytest-7.1.2 pytest-forked-1.4.0 pytest-rerunfailures-10.2 pytest-xdist-2.5.0 python-dateutil-2.8.2 pytz-2022.1 pyyaml-6.0 six-1.16.0 sortedcontainers-2.4.0 tblib-1.7.0 toml-0.10.2 tomli-2.0.1 toolz-0.11.2 tornado-6.1 typing-extensions-4.2.0 urllib3-1.26.9 virtualenv-20.14.1 zict-2.2.0
(_e) $ pytest
[…]
=========== 1 failed, 7092 passed, 875 skipped, 88 xfailed, 2691 warnings, 965 errors in 501.68s (0:08:21) ===========
# Note 965 errors due to missing conftest.py
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:

This is relevant to Linux distribution packaging, which wants to build wheels from source and run the tests, but (at least in Fedora Linux) which would prefer to use PyPI sdists instead of GitHub archives where possible.

It should be sufficient to add `onftest.py` to `MANIFEST.in`. I will follow up with a PR.

**Environment**:

- Dask version: any (2022.5.0, or current `main`)
- Python version: any (3.10.4)
- Operating System: any (Fedora Linux 36)
- Install method (conda, pip, source): PyPI sdist

testing","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Since geopandas imports parts of Pandas’s actual test suite (`pandas.tests`), not only `pandas.testing`, its tests ought to depend on the `test` extra of Pandas.

This is mostly a formality, but it could possibly avoid any practical issues with new Pandas test dependencies in the future.

[Currently](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1be9d3868f4b9cfd083839cf39eecfd881f387ad/setup.cfg#L48), this change brings in:

```ini
[options.extras_require]
test =
    hypothesis>=5.5.3
    pytest>=6.0
    pytest-xdist>=1.31
```

which adds a stricter minimum versions to the `pytest` dependency Geopandas’s tests already have, and (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependencies on `pytest-xdist` and `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not geopandas imports parts of Pandas’s actual test suite (`pandas.tests`), not only `pandas.testing`, its tests ought to depend on the `test` extra of Pandas.

This is mostly a formality, but it could possibly avoid any practical issues with new Pandas test dependencies in the future.

[Currently](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1be9d3868f4b9cfd083839cf39eecfd881f387ad/setup.cfg#L48), this change brings in:

```ini
[options.extras_require]
test =
    hypothesis>=5.5.3
    pytest>=6.0
    pytest-xdist>=1.31
```

which adds a stricter minimum versions to the `pytest` dependency Geopandas’s tests already have, and (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependencies on `pytest-xdist` and `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not ```

which adds a stricter minimum versions to the `pytest` dependency Geopandas’s tests already have, and (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependencies on `pytest-xdist` and `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not accepted.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/issues/2438/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/issues/2438/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/issues/comments/1135059443","html_url":"https://github.com/geopandas/geopandas/pull/2438#issuecomment-1135059443","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/issues/2438","id":1135059443,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAKfjv85Dp6Hz","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-23T19:29:32Z","updated_at":"2022-05-23T19:29:32Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> Personally I am fine with adding it to `requirements-dev.txt` (I don't use that file myself)
> 
> I am bit curious though, do we actually need those test requirements of pandas? For example, in my development environment, I don't have `hypothesis` installed, and never ran into problems with that (didn't check if it would work without `pytest-xdist`).

Summarizing and expanding on my comments in the PR text:

- It’s true that these extra dependencies aren’t needed for `pandas.tests.extension.base`, which is the only part of `pandas.tests` that `geopandas.tests` uses (in `geopandas/tests/test_extension_array.py`).
- The `pandas[test]` dependency might be useful if `pandas.tests.extension.base` gained new dependencies in the future, but then again you could just rely on CI and cross that bridge if you get there.
- The `pandas[test]` dependency *is* needed in Fedora Linux, since we separate `pandas.tests` from the main Pandas RPM package, and instead distribute it in the same RPM package that provides the `test` extra. However, since this is a downstream decision, we can easily keep dealing with the consequences downstream, too.

> Alternatively, should we add a `pip install geopandas[test]` test extras in geopandas itself, instead of relying on `pandas[test]` ?

Speaking as a distribution packager, a `test` extra is always nice because it separates testing dependencies from miscellaneous developer cruft like linters and formatters. I think it’s mostly orthogonal to this PR, though: adding a `test` extra moves the dependencies out of `requirements-dev.txt`, but doesn’t change what they need to be.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/geopandas/geopandas/issues/comments/1135059443/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/events","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110","id":1243963499,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAbcwm844O4Qk","number":9110,"title":"Add `pandas[test]` to `test` extra","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-05-21T12:24:17Z","updated_at":"2022-05-24T12:48:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/pulls/9110","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110","diff_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Since the tests use `pandas.test` (the actual Pandas test suite), not only `pandas.testing`, it makes sense to rely on the Pandas `test` extra.

- [x] Closes (no issue filed)
- [x] Tests added / passed
- [x] Passes `pre-commit run --all-files`

This is mostly a formality, but it could possibly avoid any practical issues with new Pandas test dependencies in the future.

[Currently](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1be9d3868f4b9cfd083839cf39eecfd881f387ad/setup.cfg#L48), this change brings in:

```ini
[options.extras_require]
test =
    hypothesis>=5.5.3
    pytest>=6.0
    pytest-xdist>=1.31
```

which adds minimum versions to the `pytest` and `pytest-xdist` dependencies Dask’s tests already have, and a (technically unnecessary in this case) test dependency on `hypothesis`.

Additionally—and this is my personal motivation for this PR—the `python-pandas` package in Fedora Linux is splitting off `pandas.tests` from the main `python3-pandas` package and installing them with `python3-pandas+test` package. So those few dependent packages that need `pandas.tests` for their own tests (currently, `python-dask` and `python-geopandas`) will have to require `pandas[test]` at build/test time, not just `pandas`. We can do this downstream in the Fedora package if this PR is not accepted.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1135879535","html_url":"https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9110#issuecomment-1135879535","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/9110","id":1135879535,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAbcwm85DtCVv","user":{"login":"musicinmybrain","id":6898909,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4OTg5MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6898909?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain","html_url":"https://github.com/musicinmybrain","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musicinmybrain/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-24T12:48:58Z","updated_at":"2022-05-24T12:48:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Thanks @musicinmybrain. Alternatively, I wonder if we could instead not rely on importing the `pandas` test suite. Looking at our existing tests, it appears we only ever import from `pandas.test` here https://github.com/dask/dask/blob/4c0cdfdbdb013cc57cd702c8d79b52f37203f8f3/dask/dataframe/tests/test_extensions.py and our actual usage is pretty minimal -- we just want some extension array objects and dtypes (in this case `DecimalArray`, `DecimalDtype`). I wonder if there is an extension arrary / dtype we can import directly from `pandas`?

I like the idea! I’m trying to understand the details well enough to figure out whether it makes sense.

Grepping the Pandas source for the `@register_extension_dtype` decorator turns up several cases in the tests, plus:

- `pandas.core.dtypes.dtypes.CategoricalDtype` / `pandas.Categorical`
- `pandas.core.dtypes.dtypes.DatetimeTZDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.DatetimeArray`
- `pandas.core.dtypes.dtypes.PeriodDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.PeriodArray`
- `pandas.core.dtypes.dtypes.IntervalDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.IntervalArray`
- `pandas.core.dtypes.dtypes.PandasDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.PandasArray` **(this one is not decorated with `@register_extension_dtype`)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.floating.Float32Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.floating.FloatingArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.floating.Float64Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.floating.FloatingArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.boolean.BooleanDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.boolean.BooleanArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.Int8Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.Int16Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.Int32Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.Int64Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.UInt8Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.UInt16Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.UInt32Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.integer.UInt64Dtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.integer.IntegerArray` **(experimental API)**
- `pandas.core.arrays.sparse.dtype.SparseDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.sparse.array.SparseArray`
- `pandas.core.arrays.string_.StringDtype` / `pandas.core.arrays.string_.StringArray` or `pandas.core.arrays.string_.ArrowStringArray`

(In general, these dtypes are exposed directly under `pandas`, and the arrays are accessible via their `construct_array_type` methods.)

Some of these are already registered in `dask` with `@make_array_nonempty.register` / `@make_scalar.register`:

- `DatetimeTZDtype`, `StringDtype`, and `BooleanDtype` in `dask.dataframe._dtypes`

These don’t seem to be (no match for `register.*FooDtype`), although I wonder if some of them may be added to `dask.dataframe._dtypes` in the future:

- `CategoricalDtype`, `PeriodDtype`, `IntervalDtype`, `PandasDtype`, `Float32Dtype`, `Float64Dtype`, `Int8Dtype`, `Int16Dtype`, `Int32Dtype`, `Int64Dtype`, `UInt8Dtype`, `UInt16Dtype`, `UInt32Dtype`, `UInt64Dtype`, `SparseDtype`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/dask/dask/issues/comments/1135879535/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This does not affect source distributions, and Cython sources (`.pyx`), definition files (`.pxd`), and include files (`.pxi`) are still installed with binary distributions.

Somewhat inclined to just keep them as is so that the source code stays intact

On Python 3.10 / Linux / x86_64, for the `.whl` file, it saves 90kiB of 35MiB, and for the uncompressed wheel contents, it saves 452kiB of 138MiB. It’s probably negligible in the context of a package as large as Pandas.

If this isn’t merged upstream—which is fine—we’ll probably still carry the patch downstream in Fedora Linux. There are other ways for users of the `python-pandas` RPM package to access the C sources (source RPMs, or `debuginfo`/`debugsource` RPMs for debugging), and we tend not to want to ship `.h` files in a package that isn’t a `-devel` package.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/comments/1096670234/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
